title: Paper Explained: DARE-bench: Evaluating Modeling and Instruction Fidelity of LLMs in Data Science - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: How AI Follows Instructions in Data Science

content.background:
Before this research, there was a big gap in how we test and improve large language models (LLMs) for data science work. As these models started taking on multi-step tasks—like cleaning data, choosing a modeling approach, and evaluating results—people needed a way to judge not just the final answer, but whether the model actually followed the given instructions and the proper workflow. Many existing benchmarks relied on human judges or vague criteria, which could be inconsistent or biased. They often didn’t check the step-by-step process, so a model might appear smart by producing a plausible final result even if it didn’t truly adhere to the required procedure. This made it hard to compare models fairly or to trust that improvements reflected real, reliable behavior.

Another problem was training data. To teach LLMs to follow data science instructions well, researchers need lots of high-quality examples that clearly show the correct way to proceed. Without accurately labeled data and clear ground truth, it’s easy to misjudge a model’s learning or to overfit to specific tasks. In short, there was a bottleneck: unreliable evaluation plus scarce, well-labeled training material made it difficult to measure progress and to train models that genuinely follow data science workflows. The motivation behind this work is to create a benchmark with objective, verifiable answers drawn from real-world tasks, so researchers can reliably gauge instruction adherence and modeling ability and have solid data to train and fine-tune future models.
content.methodology:
DARE-bench is designed to fix two big gaps in how we evaluate LLMs for data science: (1) you want to know not just if a model gives a correct final answer, but whether it follows the intended data-science workflow (the steps you’d actually perform), and (2) you want labeled data that truly reflects correct solutions. The authors create a benchmark with verifiable ground truth across thousands of tasks, drawn from Kaggle. The idea is to test both the modeling skills (building good models) and instruction-following skills (adhering to the prescribed process and using tools as intended). Think of it like a cooking test where you’re not only judged on the taste of the dish, but also on whether you followed the recipe and used the right kitchen tools.

How they do it, in simple steps:
- Build the task set with clear ground truth: they take real Kaggle problems and attach a canonical solution path and a correct final outcome. This gives you an objective “answer key” to compare against.
- Make process fidelity measurable: beyond the final results, the benchmark checks whether the model followed the prescribed data-science steps (the right modeling approach, feature choices, evaluation metrics, and even tool usage). The ground-truth setup lets you score adherence consistently, not by a human judging vibe.
- Provide training data and evaluation splits: there’s large-scale training data derived from these tasks to fine-tune models, plus separate evaluation sets to fairly measure performance on unseen tasks.
- Support agentic tool use: many tasks are designed so that solving them can involve interactive tools (like running code, manipulating data, or using modeling libraries), which lets the benchmark assess how models handle real, tool-enabled workflows.
- Run and compare experiments: researchers can compare models with and without fine-tuning, and quantify gains in both final accuracy and instruction-following fidelity. The paper reports substantial improvements from supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning on these tasks.

What this achieves conceptually: DARE-bench provides a reliable, objective way to evaluate whether an LLM can both reason about data science problems and faithfully execute the prescribed workflow. Because every task has a ground-truth solution, scoring is reproducible and free of subjective judge opinions. It also creates a rich training resource: models can learn from many real-world data-science tasks, and the benchmark shows that training on these tasks can yield big performance boosts—e.g., notable gains from supervised fine-tuning and from reinforcement learning on different model sizes. In short, DARE-bench is not just a test of “smarts” but a test of following the right process to solve data science problems, trained and validated on a large, real-world task library.
content.results:
DARE-bench is a new benchmark designed to test how well large language models (LLMs) can follow real data science instructions and actually build models in a step-by-step way. It plugs two gaps researchers saw: first, many benchmarks don’t check whether a model sticks to the intended process (like following the right modeling steps), and second, there isn’t enough high-quality labeled data to train or evaluate these systems. To fix this, DARE-bench assembles about 6,300 tasks that come from Kaggle—real, practical data science problems—with clear ground-truth answers. This means every task has a correct solution path and result that can be used to judge models consistently, without needing human judgment.

Compared to older benchmarks, DARE-bench emphasizes process fidelity and objective scoring, not just final answers. Many previous tests relied on humans or indirect criteria to say whether a model was doing well, which can be subjective and hard to reproduce. The results show that even very capable LLMs still struggle with authentic data science modeling tasks, especially when it comes to following proper modeling workflows. That highlights a gap between current model capabilities and the kind of careful, multi-step reasoning real data science work demands.

The practical impact is twofold: DARE-bench isn’t just a test—it's also a rich training resource. Fine-tuning models on the DARE-bench data improves their performance, with supervised fine-tuning helping some models and reinforcement learning-based fine-tuning giving large boosts for others. This demonstrates that high-quality, process-focused training data can meaningfully teach models to adhere to data science workflows and produce better, more reliable results. In short, DARE-bench provides a realistic way to measure progress and a powerful way to train LLMs to be more trustworthy data science assistants.
content.significance:
DARE-bench matters today because it tackles a very real bottleneck: how do we know an LLM will actually follow a data science workflow and produce trustworthy results, not just clever text? The paper creates a large, objective testing ground for data-science tasks by using 6,300 Kaggle-derived problems with verifiable ground truth. That means scoring is automatic and reproducible, not left to crowds or ad-hoc judgments. The finding that even strong models struggle with core data-science modeling tasks, and that targeted training data can yield big performance gains (for example, supervised tuning boosting Qwen3-32B by about 1.8x and RLHF boosting Qwen3-4B by more than 8x), strongly argues that we need both high-quality task datasets and careful instruction-following training to get LLMs to reliably handle real-work pipelines.

In the long run, DARE-bench has helped shape how we evaluate AI for data science. It pushes for process-aware evaluation—not just whether the final answer is correct, but whether the model adheres to the correct data-analysis steps and follows domain rules. This idea of instruction and process fidelity has influenced later work on domain-specific benchmarks and the data papers used to fine-tune and align models for specialized tasks. The impact extends beyond academic benchmarks: it underpins how modern AI assistants embedded in real tools are trained and evaluated. Think of data-science notebook copilots, Kaggle-style AI teammates, and AutoML platforms that aim to guide users through modeling pipelines with verifiable steps. The principles also echo in the way big systems today (including ChatGPT-style assistants and their code interpreters) are increasingly tuned with domain-specific data and rigorous evaluation to improve reliability and safety.

Connecting to what people use every day, DARE-bench helps explain why today’s AI like ChatGPT and coding assistants can sometimes falter on multi-step data tasks unless they’re trained on high-quality, domain-specific data. Its emphasis on objective scoring and real-world task coverage provides a blueprint for building trustworthy data-science copilots—tools that can not only give you results but also follow the right workflow and provide reproducible, ground-truth-backed outcomes. For university students, the lasting message is clear: as AI becomes more embedded in data work, benchmarking that captures both the right answers and the right process will be the backbone of lasting reliability and real-world usefulness.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Verifiable ground truth: The Heart of DARE-bench
concept_explanation.content:
Imagine you’re giving someone a test and you have an exact answer key. Verifiable ground truth works the same way for benchmarking AI: there is a fixed, checkable correct answer or outcome for every task. In DARE-bench, every task has this ground truth so you can automatically verify whether an AI’s response is good or bad, without needing a human judge to decide. This makes comparisons fair, objective, and repeatable.

How does it work, step by step? First, researchers collect a large set of tasks from Kaggle, a platform full of real data science problems. Each task comes with a defined goal (for example, predict a label, optimize a metric, or follow a data-science instruction). Crucially, there is a ground truth that can be checked automatically—such as the true labels on a hold-out test set or a programmatic rubric that can calculate a score from the model’s output. Second, they build an evaluation pipeline where the AI is prompted or asked to perform the task, its output is captured, and a predefined metric compares the output to the ground truth. Because the ground truth is verifiable, the score is objective, not subject to a human judge’s opinion.

To make this concrete, consider two kinds of tasks in DARE-bench. For a modeling task (like a Kaggle competition), the ground truth might be the actual labels or the best possible predictions on a test set, so the system can compute error or accuracy automatically. For an instruction-following task (where the goal is to demonstrate a correct data science workflow), the ground truth could be a gold-standard solution path or a set of automatic checks (unit tests or rubric-driven checks) that verify whether the model’s steps meet the required criteria. In both cases, there is an exact, machine-checkable criterion that determines whether the model did the task correctly.

Why is this important? Verifiable ground truth removes ambiguity. Many benchmarks rely on human judgments or model-based judges, which can introduce subjectivity, inconsistency, or bias. With verifiable ground truth, you can reproduce results exactly and fairly compare different models or training approaches. It also makes it feasible to scale up evaluation: you can automatically score thousands of tasks without hiring more human raters. This reliability is especially valuable for measuring how well models follow instructions and maintain process fidelity, not just produce a final answer.

In practice, this enables several useful applications. Researchers can build robust benchmarks to gauge how well a model handles data science tasks end-to-end, from data handling to prediction and interpretation. Developers can use verifiable ground truth to create training data and fine-tuning routines, knowing that improvements are measured against an objective standard. Education and tool-building benefit too: students can learn with problems that have clear, checkable solutions, and engineers can compare different LLMs or agentic tools with confidence. Overall, verifiable ground truth makes benchmarking more trustworthy, scalable, and useful for advancing how AI assists with real-world data science work.

summary: This paper introduced DARE-bench, a ground-truth, process-aware benchmark with 6,300 Kaggle-derived data science tasks that enables objective evaluation and targeted training of LLMs, demonstrating that fine-tuning or reinforcement learning can substantially boost model performance.
